title: Rate Changes in Flood Occurences
text:
HaHaHa

library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.1
✔ ggplot2   4.0.0     ✔ tibble    3.3.0
✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
✔ purrr     1.1.0     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

library(readr)


# Read Data Sets  ----


floods_per_year = read_tsv("Data/floods_per_year.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
max_year= max(floods_per_year$varve_year)


## Definition of some helper data files ----
years = 1:max_year

x <- rep(NA, max_year)
x[floods_per_year$varve_year] <- floods_per_year$no_of_layers

x_data = rep(1,max_year)
x_data[is.na(x)] = 0
x[is.na(x)] <- 0


bandwidth = 250
filter_coeffs <- dnorm(seq(-4,4,by=1./bandwidth)) 
length_filter <- length(filter_coeffs)

## Getting prepared for fast filtering: zero padding at the beginning and end of the files ----
xx <- c(rep(0,length_filter), x, rep(0,length_filter))
xx_data <- c(rep(0,length_filter), x_data, rep(0,length_filter))

## Filter the data with a Gaussian kernel ----
# (note that the resulting  vectors have the original  (before padding with zeros) length) 

no_of_floods_per_bandwidth  <- (stats::filter(xx, filter_coeffs))[length_filter + (1:length(x))]
effective_no_of_data_points <-(stats::filter(xx_data, filter_coeffs))[length_filter + (1:length(x))]

flood_rate_bw_250yrs = no_of_floods_per_bandwidth/effective_no_of_data_points

## Computation of confidence bands ----
CI_l = 0.5*qchisq(0.025,2*no_of_floods_per_bandwidth)/effective_no_of_data_points
CI_u = 0.5*qchisq(0.975,2*no_of_floods_per_bandwidth+2)/effective_no_of_data_points

# PLOT ----

## Tibble for flood density Plot ----
rate_floods_per_century <- tibble(time = years, flood_rate_bw_250yrs = 100 * flood_rate_bw_250yrs, lower_confidence_interval = 100 * CI_l, upper_confidence_interval = 100 * CI_u) 
  
## Plot of Upper Panel ----
w = which(floods_per_year$no_of_layers >0)
rep(floods_per_year$varve_year[w], floods_per_year$no_of_layers[w]) -> flood_years
flood_years = tibble(time = flood_years, no_of_layers = 0)

ggplot() +
  geom_ribbon(data = rate_floods_per_century, aes(x = time, ymin = lower_confidence_interval, ymax = upper_confidence_interval), fill = "darkgrey", alpha=0.5) +
  geom_line(data = rate_floods_per_century, aes(x = time, y = flood_rate_bw_250yrs), col = "#d8727aff" ) +
  geom_hline( yintercept = 8.3, col = "#d8727aff", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_rug(data = flood_years, aes(x = time, y = no_of_layers), sides = "t", length = unit(0.05, "npc"), lwd = 0.25, position = "jitter", alpha = 0.5, color = "darkgrey") +
  geom_rect(data = rate_floods_per_century, aes(x = time, ymin = lower_confidence_interval, ymax = upper_confidence_interval), xmin = 4019, xmax = 4083, ymin = 0, ymax = 32., col = "#646868", fill = "#646868") +
  annotate( 'text', x = 6100, y = 18.5, label = "Average Flood Rate: 8.3 Floods per 100 yr",  col = "#d8727aff", size = 3.5, vjust = 0, hjust = 0 ) + 
  annotate( 'segment', x = 7350, y = 18, xend = 7350, yend = 8.8, col = "#d8727aff", linewidth = 0.5, arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, 'cm'), angle = 22.5, type = "closed")) +
  annotate( 'text', x = 5300, y = 0.5, label = "65 year gap",  size = 3.5, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 0 ) + 
  annotate( 'segment', x = 5200, y = 0.5, xend = 4150, yend = 0.5, linewidth = 0.5, arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, 'cm'), angle = 22.5, type = "closed")) +
#coord_cartesian(clip = "off") + 
  labs( x ="Relative Age [yr]", 
       y = " ", 
       title = "Time-Dependent Flood Rates with 95%-Confidence Intervals",  
       subtitle = "for the Reconstructed Paleofloods from Pianico Sellere Basin", 
      
       caption = paste("Dr. Annette Witt, Origami Data Science Services\n ",format(Sys.Date(), format = "%d. %B %Y"),"\n Data Source: www.pangaea.de")) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-200, 9500), expand = c(0,0), breaks = (0:4)*2000) + 
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = (0:3)*10, limits = c(0, 32), expand = c(NA,0)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
        panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank(),
        legend.position = "none")

title: Rate Changes in Event Series
text:
Event series are time series that record the number of events within equally-sized time intervals. They serve as models for neuronal spike trains, hydrogeological extreme events such as earthquakes or floods, or the occurrence of ectopic heartbeats in an otherwise healthy heart rhythm. Event series can have constant or time-dependent event rates. How can these two cases be distinguished?
Let’s consider two examples and start with an event series with a constant rate. The data set has a large observational gap in the time interval (4000, 5500).

library(tidyverse)


set.seed(3)
events <- rpois(10000, 0.05)
event_series <- tibble(x = 1:length(events), y = events)
is.na(event_series$y) <- c(4000:5500)
cols <- c("Events" = "black", "Spline" = "red", "Constant" = "blue")
events <- event_series |> filter(y > 0)
ggplot() +
     geom_rect(aes(xmin = 4000, xmax = 5500, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf), color = NA, fill = "olivedrab", alpha = 0.3) +
     geom_rug(data = events, aes(x = x, col = "Events"), lwd = 0.05, show.legend = FALSE) +
     geom_point(data = events[1,], aes(x = x, y = y, col = "Events"), shape = 124, size = 5) +
     geom_smooth(data = event_series, aes(x = x, y = y, col = "Spline"), formula = "y ~ x", method = "loess", lty = "dashed",  na.rm = TRUE, alpha = 0.5, linewidth = 0.5) +
     geom_smooth(data = event_series, method = "lm",  na.rm = TRUE, formula = "y ~ 1", aes(x = x, y = y, col = "Constant"), se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.5) +
     annotate("text", label = "No Data", col = "white", x = 4500, y = 0.05, size = 8, angle = 90) +
     coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0.02, 0.08)) +
     guides(col = guide_legend(override.aes = list(
       shape    = c(NA, NA, 124),
       linetype = c("solid", "dashed", "blank"),
       linewidth = c(0.5, 0.5, 0),
       size     = c(NA, NA, 4)
     ))) +
     scale_color_manual(values = cols, breaks = c("Constant", "Spline", "Events")) +
     theme_minimal() +
     labs(
       x = "Time",
       y = "Event Rate",
       title = "Event Rates for a Poisson Process",
       col = "Data / Models",
       caption = paste("Dr. Annette Witt\n Origami Data Science Services\n", format(Sys.Date(), format = "%d. %B %Y"))
     ) +
     theme(
       panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank(),
       panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
     )




Estimated time-dependent event rate (red dashed line) with confidence band (grey) and the estimated constant rate (blue line); rug plot of events shown at the bottom.




The plot shows a rug plot of the event series at the bottom, and the estimated time-dependent rate (red dashed line) with its 95% confidence band (grey) and the estimated constant rate (blue line). The estimated rate is more uncertain at the beginning and the end of the event series and in the vicinity of the observational gap, i.e., the confidence band widens.
For event series with time-dependent (here periodically changing) rates, however, the following features are likely to be found:

set.seed(1)
len <- 10000
rate <- tibble(rate = 0.05 + 0.025 * sin(0.0005 * 2 * pi * (1:len)))
event_series <- rate |>
  mutate(x = row_number(), y = map_dbl(rate, \(x) rpois(1, x)))
events <- event_series |> filter(y > 0)

ggplot() +
     geom_rug(data = events, aes(x = x, col = "Events"), lwd = 0.05, show.legend = FALSE) +
     geom_point(data = events[1,], aes(x = x, y = y, col = "Events"), shape = 124, size = 5) +
     geom_smooth(data = event_series, aes(x = x, y = y, col = "Spline"), method = "loess", formula = "y ~ x", span = 0.1, lty = "dashed", alpha = 0.5, linewidth = 0.5) +
     geom_smooth(data = event_series, method = "lm", formula = "y ~ 1", aes(x = x, y = y, col = "Constant"), se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.5) +
     guides(col = guide_legend(override.aes = list(
       shape    = c(NA, NA, 124),
       linetype = c("solid", "dashed", "blank"),
       linewidth = c(0.5, 0.5, 0),
       size     = c(NA, NA, 4)
     ))) +
     coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.05, 0.14), expand = FALSE) +
     scale_color_manual(values = cols, breaks = c("Constant", "Spline", "Events")) +
     theme_minimal() +
     labs(
       x = "Time",
       y = "Event Rate",
       title = "Event Rates for a Poisson Process with Time-Dependent Rate",
       col = "Data / Models",
       caption = paste("Dr. Annette Witt\n Origami Data Science Services\n", format(Sys.Date(), format = "%d. %B %Y"))
     ) +
     theme(
       panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank(),
       panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
     )




The graph represents the same analysis as above, here for an event series with a non-constant event rate.




Here the constant rate (blue line) periodically lays outside the confidence band of the time-dependent rate (grey area).

title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
This is the first post in a Quarto blog. Welcome!

Since this post doesn’t specify an explicit image, the first image in the post will be used in the listing page of posts.

title: ECGs of Fibrillating Hearts
text:
You’re probably familiar with what an ECG looks like under normal (sinus) rhythm. Ventricular fibrillation, however, is a very different signal: highly irregular, nonstationary, and difficult to characterize. Clinically, it’s one of the most critical cardiac arrhythmias—fatal within minutes if untreated.
In our recent paper (https://doi.org/10.3389/frsip.2025.1707422), we analyze ECG recordings from fibrillating murine hearts from a time series perspective. I have written the accompanying R package FibFreq with methods for estimating the fibrillation frequency and which includes a data set of 112 ECG recordings. Below, I visualize a small selection of these signals.
Each time series is one second long, sampled at 1 kHz (1,000 data points). To reduce 50 Hz power line noise, we applied a fifth-order Butterworth low-pass filter with a 45 Hz cutoff.

if(!require(tidyverse)) install.packages("tidyverse")
if(!require(FibFreq)){
   library(devtools)
    install_github("origami-dss/FibFreq")}
library(tidyverse)
library(FibFreq)


ecgs_selected  <- c(1, 37, 112, 13)
minitib <- function(no, i) {
  tibble(no = rep(no, 1000), 
         time = 1:1000, 
         ecg = MiceFibECGs[,i], 
         orig_no = rep(i, 1000)) }

map2_dfr(1:length(ecgs_selected), ecgs_selected, minitib) -> data_fig_ecgs

data_fig_ecgs_text <- data.frame(
  no   = c(1,2,3,4),
  label = paste("ECG No.",ecgs_selected))

data_fig_ecgs_y_scales <- data.frame(
  no   = c(1,1,2,2,3,3,4,4),
  time = rep(0,8),
  ecg = c(-0.1, 0.2, -0.03, 0.03, -0.03, 0.03, -0.01, 0.02))

data_fig_ecgs %>%
  ggplot(aes(x=time, y=ecg)) +
  geom_line(col="darkblue", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_blank(data = data_fig_ecgs_y_scales) +
  facet_grid(rows = vars(no), scales = "free") +
  geom_text(data = data_fig_ecgs_text, 
            mapping = aes(x = 0, y = Inf, label = label, hjust = 0, vjust = 1 ), 
            fontface = "bold", size = 3) +
  labs( x = "Time [ms]", title = "Examples of Murine ECGs (Source: R Package FibFreq) \n",  
        caption = paste("Dr. Annette Witt\n Origami Data Science Services\n", format(Sys.Date(), format = "%d. %B %Y"))) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0)), breaks = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits= c(-01, 1020),  expand =  c(0, 0), breaks = (0:5)*200) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(strip.text.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank(),
        plot.margin = margin(t = 5,  # Top margin
                             r = 5,  # Right margin
                             b = 5,  # Bottom margin
                             l = 20)
  )




ECGs signals of murine fibrillating hearts.




All four time series exhibit oscillatory behavior, with an apparent increase in irregularity from top to bottom. Intuitively, one would expect a more reliable estimate of the fundamental frequency for the topmost ECG, and a less certain estimate for the ECG shown in the bottom panel. The FibFreq package provides four time-domain and three frequency-domain techniques for estimating the fundamental frequency of a time series. In addition, it outputs quantities that characterize the irregularity of the oscillations .

title: Event Series Analysis
text:
Event series are time series that record the number of events within equally-sized time intervals. They serve as models for neuronal spike trains, hydrogeological extreme events such as earthquakes or floods, or the occurrence of ectopic heartbeats in an otherwise healthy heart rhythm. Event series can have constant or time-dependent event rates. How can these two cases be distinguished?
Let’s consider two examples and start with an event series with a constant rate. The data set has a large observational gap in the time interval (4000, 5500).

library(tidyverse)


set.seed(3)
events <- rpois(10000, 0.05)
event_series <- tibble(x = 1:length(events), y = events)
is.na(event_series$y) <- c(4000:5500)
cols <- c("Events" = "black", "Spline" = "red", "Constant" = "blue")
events <- event_series |> filter(y > 0)
ggplot() +
     geom_rect(aes(xmin = 4000, xmax = 5500, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf), color = NA, fill = "olivedrab", alpha = 0.3) +
     geom_rug(data = events, aes(x = x, col = "Events"), lwd = 0.05, show.legend = FALSE) +
     geom_point(data = events[1,], aes(x = x, y = y, col = "Events"), shape = 124, size = 5) +
     geom_smooth(data = event_series, aes(x = x, y = y, col = "Spline"), formula = "y ~ x", method = "loess", lty = "dashed",  na.rm = TRUE, alpha = 0.5, linewidth = 0.5) +
     geom_smooth(data = event_series, method = "lm",  na.rm = TRUE, formula = "y ~ 1", aes(x = x, y = y, col = "Constant"), se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.5) +
     annotate("text", label = "No Data", col = "white", x = 4500, y = 0.05, size = 8, angle = 90) +
     coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0.02, 0.08)) +
     guides(col = guide_legend(override.aes = list(
       shape    = c(NA, NA, 124),
       linetype = c("solid", "dashed", "blank"),
       linewidth = c(0.5, 0.5, 0),
       size     = c(NA, NA, 4) 
     ))) +
     scale_color_manual(values = cols, breaks = c("Constant", "Spline", "Events")) +
     theme_minimal() +
     labs(
       x = "Time",
       y = "Event Rate",
       title = "Event Rates for a Poisson Process\n",
       col = "Data / Models",
       caption = paste("Dr. Annette Witt\n Origami Data Science Services\n", format(Sys.Date(), format = "%d. %B %Y"))
     ) +
     theme(
       panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank(),
       panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(), 
       plot.margin = margin(t = 10, r = 5, b = 2,  l = 5)
     )




Estimated time-dependent event rate (red dashed line) with confidence band (grey) and the estimated constant rate (blue line); rug plot of events shown at the bottom.




The plot shows a rug plot of the event series at the bottom, and the estimated time-dependent rate (red dashed line) with its 95% confidence band (grey) and the estimated constant rate (blue line). The estimated rate is more uncertain at the beginning and the end of the event series and in the vicinity of the observational gap, i.e., the confidence band widens.
For event series with time-dependent (here periodically changing) rates, however, the following features are likely to be found:

set.seed(1)
len <- 10000
rate <- tibble(rate = 0.05 + 0.025 * sin(0.0005 * 2 * pi * (1:len)))
event_series <- rate |>
  mutate(x = row_number(), y = map_dbl(rate, \(x) rpois(1, x)))
events <- event_series |> filter(y > 0)

ggplot() +
     geom_rug(data = events, aes(x = x, col = "Events"), lwd = 0.05, show.legend = FALSE) +
     geom_point(data = events[1,], aes(x = x, y = y, col = "Events"), shape = 124, size = 5) +
     geom_smooth(data = event_series, aes(x = x, y = y, col = "Spline"), method = "loess", formula = "y ~ x", span = 0.1, lty = "dashed", alpha = 0.5, linewidth = 0.5) +
     geom_smooth(data = event_series, method = "lm", formula = "y ~ 1", aes(x = x, y = y, col = "Constant"), se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.5) +
     guides(col = guide_legend(override.aes = list(
       shape    = c(NA, NA, 124),
       linetype = c("solid", "dashed", "blank"),
       linewidth = c(0.5, 0.5, 0),
       size     = c(NA, NA, 4)
     ))) +
     coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.05, 0.14), expand = FALSE) +
     scale_color_manual(values = cols, breaks = c("Constant", "Spline", "Events")) +
     theme_minimal() +
     labs(
       x = "Time",
       y = "Event Rate",
       title = "Event Rates for a Poisson Process with Time-Dependent Rate",
       col = "Data / Models",
       caption = paste("Dr. Annette Witt\n Origami Data Science Services\n", format(Sys.Date(), format = "%d. %B %Y"))
     ) +
     theme(
       panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank(),
       panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
     )




The graph represents the same analysis as above, here for an event series with a non-constant event rate.




Here the constant rate (blue line) periodically lays outside the confidence band of the time-dependent rate (grey area).

title: Fundamental Frequency of the Sunspot Data
text:
How to compute the frequency of an oscillatory time series? And what can you do if this oscillation is irregular? In our recent paper (https://doi.org/10.3389/frsip.2025.1707422), we proposed and discussed four time domain techniques and three frequency domain techniques for doing that. They are provided in the accompanying R package FibFreq. Below I will briefly explain two of these techniques. Their functionality is illustrated for the solar cycle time series . We begin with loading the sunspot data set and smoothing it:

if(!require(tidyverse)) install.packages("tidyverse")
if(!require(FibFreq)){
   library(devtools)
    install_github("origami-dss/FibFreq")}
library(tidyverse)
library(FibFreq)

# We load the monthly  sunspot data set
sunspots <- as.vector(sunspot.month)

# which has a sampling interval of 
delta_t <- 1./12

# As the time series is very noisy, we apply a 3-year moving average filter
filtered_sunspots <- stats::filter(sunspots, rep(1./36,36))

# This leads to leading and ending values NA. 
# As the FibFreq library does not handle them, they have to be removed.
smoothed_sunspots <- filtered_sunspots[!is.na(filtered_sunspots)]

#names(smoothed_sunspots) <-"sunspots"

We begin with identifying the local maxima of this time series and consider the average temporal distance between successive maxima as a the fundamental period.

# The parameters of the maximum identification function have to be adjusted 
freq_M2M <- freq_max2max(smoothed_sunspots, delta_t = delta_t, span = 31)

time = (1:length(smoothed_sunspots)) * delta_t
timings_maxima_Max2Max = tibble(time = freq_M2M$maxima_sampled * delta_t, 
                                sunspots = smoothed_sunspots[freq_M2M$maxima_sampled])
tibble(time = (1:length(smoothed_sunspots)) * delta_t, sunspots = smoothed_sunspots) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = time, y = sunspots)) +
  geom_line(col = "darkgreen") +
  geom_point(data = timings_maxima_Max2Max, aes(x = time, y = sunspots), col = "darkred") + 
  labs( x = "Time [month]", y = " ", 
        title = "Smoothed Sunspot Data with Identified Maxima", 
        caption = paste("Dr. Annette Witt\n Origami Data Science Services\n", format(Sys.Date(), format = "%d. %B %Y"))) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(strip.text.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank(), 
        plot.margin = margin(t = 5,  # Top margin
                             r = 5,  # Right margin
                             b = 5,  # Bottom margin
                             l = 20))








The fundamental period based on max-to-max distances corresponds to a period of 1./freq_M2M$freq_max2max = 10.9 years which is very close to the expected 11-year cycle. The coefficient of variation of the intervals between successive maxima is freq_M2M$c_v = 0.16, or, equivalently the standard deviation of the max-to-max distances is 1.7 years which indicates a moderate level of irregularity of the oscillation.
The fundamental frequency can also be estimated by spectral techniques:

freq_MaxP <- freq_argmax_periodogram(smoothed_sunspots,
                                     delta_t = delta_t,
                                     f_min = 0.001,
                                     f_max = 0.3)
freq_Lorentz <- freq_Lorentz_fit(smoothed_sunspots,
                                 delta_t = delta_t,
                                 f_min = 0.001,
                                 f_max = 0.3)

tibble_Lorentz_plot <- as_tibble(freq_Lorentz$Lorentz_model)


tibble_Lorentz_plot %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Frequency)) +
  geom_linerange( aes(ymin = 0, ymax = Fourier_Amps), col = "darkgreen", linewidth = 1.5) +
  geom_line( aes(y = modelled_Fourier_Amps), col = "darkorange", linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "Frequency [1/yr]", 
       title = "Smoothed Sunspot Data: Fourier Amplitudes and Lorentz Model", 
       caption = paste("Dr. Annette Witt\n Origami Data Science Services\n", format(Sys.Date(), format = "%d. %B %Y"))) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    strip.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.title.y=element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank()
  )








The largest Fourier amplitude is found at \(f=0.092\) which corresponds to a period of \(P = 10.9\) yr. If a Lorentz model is fitted to the Fourier amplitudes, it is characterized by a Lorentz frequency of \(f_{\text{Lorentz}} = 0.093\) yr-1 (\(P_{\text{Lorentz}} = 10.7\) yr) along with a Lorentz scale parameter of \(\sigma_{\text{Lorentz}} =0.011\) yr-1 indicating that the underlying oscillation is moderately irregular.

title: Comparison of Statistical Techniques
text:
In data science we are often confronted with different estimators for the same quantity. For instance, there are various methodes for calculating the fundamental freqeuncy of an oscillatory time series or for the strength of long-range persistence of a noisy time series. It is common practise to benchmark the different estimators for a specific suite of test data. How to compare the results? In this post, I will present three vizualizations of the statistical summary.
As test data I will use realizations of a model for stochastic oscillations. Each time series contains 1000 data points, the model frequency is 20 Hz. sampled at 1 kHz (1,000 data points).

if(!require(tidyverse)) install.packages("tidyverse")
if(!require(FibFreq)){
   library(devtools)
    install_github("origami-dss/FibFreq")}
library(tidyverse)
library(FibFreq)

First I define a function that computes realizations of stochastic oscillators:

Gaussian_distributed_ts_with_Gaussian_PSD <- function(T = 1, f_mean = 22.5, f_sigma = 2, delta_t = 0.001, seed = NULL)
{ 
  fs  <- 1./delta_t
  N <- T/delta_t
  N_2 <- floor(N/2)

  set.seed(seed)
  x <- rnorm(N)
  X <- fft(x, inverse = FALSE)
  freq <-  (0:N_2)/N/ delta_t

  coeffs_FourAmps <- sqrt(dnorm(freq, f_mean, f_sigma))
  if (N %% 2 == 0) multiplyers = c( coeffs_FourAmps , (rev( coeffs_FourAmps ))[-c(1, length( coeffs_FourAmps ))]) else multiplyers = c(coeffs_FourAmps, rev(coeffs_FourAmps))

  X_f <- multiplyers * X
  x_ts <- Re(fft(X_f, inverse = TRUE)/length(X_f))
}

In a second step I apply this function for creating 500 time series. For all of them the fundamental frequency is computed with five different estimators (based on functions of the FibFreq package).

N <- 500
model_data <- map_dfc(
  .x = 1:N,
  .f = function(x) {
    out <- Gaussian_distributed_ts_with_Gaussian_PSD(seed = x)
    setNames(as.data.frame(out), paste0("ts_", x))
  }
)

fn <- function(x, delta_t = 0.001, f_min = 1, f_max = 45) {
  res_sin_fit <- freq_fitted_sinusoidals(x, delta_t = delta_t, test_freqs = seq(f_min, f_max, by = 0.1))
  res_max2max <- freq_max2max(x, delta_t = delta_t)
  res_thresh <- freq_threshold_crossing(x, delta_t)
  res_MaxP <-  freq_argmax_periodogram(x, delta_t, f_min = f_min, f_max = f_max)
  names(res_MaxP)[1] <- "freq_MaxP"
  res_Lorentz <- freq_Lorentz_fit(x, delta_t = delta_t, f_min = f_min, f_max = f_max)
  return (c(res_sin_fit[1], res_max2max[1], res_thresh[1], res_MaxP[1],  res_Lorentz[1]))
}

result_frequency_estimates <- as_tibble(model_data) %>%  map_df(~ fn(.x))

freq_res_for_boxplot <- as_tibble(cbind(no = 1:N, result_frequency_estimates)) %>% pivot_longer(cols = !no,names_to = "names", values_to = "freq_estimate")


library("scico")
COLS = scico(5, palette = 'oleron')

freq_res_for_boxplot  %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = freq_estimate, y = names, fill = names)) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 22.5), col = "grey50", linewidth = 2, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.001, height = 0.1, size = 0.5, col = "grey60", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, width = 0.6, outliers = FALSE, size = 0.2, fill = COLS) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom='point', shape = 18, size = 3) + 
  labs(x = "Estimated Frequency (Hz)") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(15, 25, by = 5), minor_breaks = seq(16, 32, by = 1)) +
  scale_y_discrete(
  labels = c("freq_threshold_crossing" = expression(f[theta]), 
             "freq_MaxP" = expression(f[MaxP]), 
             "freq_max2max" = expression(f[Max2Max]), 
             "freq_Lorentz" = expression(f[Lorentz]), 
             "freq_fitted" = expression(f[BestFitting]) )) + 
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.title.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_text(hjust = 0),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 6),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank(), #
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
  )

title: Fundamental Frequency of the Sunspot Data
text:
How to compute the frequency of an oscillatory time series? And what can you do if this oscillation is irregular? In our recent paper (https://doi.org/10.3389/frsip.2025.1707422), we proposed and discussed four time domain techniques and three frequency domain techniques for doing that. They are provided in the accompanying R package FibFreq. Below I will briefly explain two of these techniques. Their functionality is illustrated for the solar cycle time series . We begin with loading the sunspot data set and smoothing it:

if(!require(tidyverse)) install.packages("tidyverse")
if(!require(FibFreq)){
   library(devtools)
    install_github("origami-dss/FibFreq")}
library(tidyverse)
library(FibFreq)

# We load the monthly  sunspot data set
sunspots <- as.vector(sunspot.month)

# which has a sampling interval of 
delta_t <- 1./12

# As the time series is very noisy, we apply a 3-year moving average filter
filtered_sunspots <- stats::filter(sunspots, rep(1./36,36))

# This leads to leading and ending values NA. 
# As the FibFreq library does not handle them, they have to be removed.
smoothed_sunspots <- filtered_sunspots[!is.na(filtered_sunspots)]

#names(smoothed_sunspots) <-"sunspots"

We begin with identifying the local maxima of this time series and consider the average temporal distance between successive maxima as a the fundamental period.

# The parameters of the maximum identification function have to be adjusted 
freq_M2M <- freq_max2max(smoothed_sunspots, delta_t = delta_t, span = 31)

time = (1:length(smoothed_sunspots)) * delta_t
timings_maxima_Max2Max = tibble(time = freq_M2M$maxima_sampled * delta_t, 
                                sunspots = smoothed_sunspots[freq_M2M$maxima_sampled])
tibble(time = (1:length(smoothed_sunspots)) * delta_t, sunspots = smoothed_sunspots) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = time, y = sunspots)) +
  geom_line(col = "darkgreen") +
  geom_point(data = timings_maxima_Max2Max, aes(x = time, y = sunspots), col = "darkred") + 
  labs( x = "Time [month]", y = " ", 
        title = "Smoothed Sunspot Data with Identified Maxima", 
        caption = paste("Dr. Annette Witt\n Origami Data Science Services\n", format(Sys.Date(), format = "%d. %B %Y"))) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(strip.text.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank(), 
        plot.margin = margin(t = 5,  # Top margin
                             r = 5,  # Right margin
                             b = 5,  # Bottom margin
                             l = 20))








The fundamental period based on max-to-max distances corresponds to a period of 1./freq_M2M$freq_max2max = 10.9 years which is very close to the expected 11-year cycle. The coefficient of variation of the intervals between successive maxima is freq_M2M$c_v = 0.16, or, equivalently the standard deviation of the max-to-max distances is 1.7 years which indicates a moderate level of irregularity of the oscillation.
The fundamental frequency can also be estimated by spectral techniques:

freq_MaxP <- freq_argmax_periodogram(smoothed_sunspots,
                                     delta_t = delta_t,
                                     f_min = 0.001,
                                     f_max = 0.3)
freq_Lorentz <- freq_Lorentz_fit(smoothed_sunspots,
                                 delta_t = delta_t,
                                 f_min = 0.001,
                                 f_max = 0.3)

tibble_Lorentz_plot <- as_tibble(freq_Lorentz$Lorentz_model)


tibble_Lorentz_plot %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Frequency)) +
  geom_linerange( aes(ymin = 0, ymax = Fourier_Amps), col = "darkgreen", linewidth = 1.5) +
  geom_line( aes(y = modelled_Fourier_Amps), col = "darkorange", linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "Frequency [1/yr]", 
       title = "Smoothed Sunspot Data: Fourier Amplitudes and Lorentz Model", 
       caption = paste("Dr. Annette Witt\n Origami Data Science Services\n", format(Sys.Date(), format = "%d. %B %Y"))) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    strip.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.title.y=element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank()
  )








The largest Fourier amplitude is found at \(f=0.092\) which corresponds to a period of \(P = 10.9\) yr. If a Lorentz model is fitted to the Fourier amplitudes, it is characterized by a Lorentz frequency of \(f_{\text{Lorentz}} = 0.093\) yr-1 (\(P_{\text{Lorentz}} = 10.7\) yr) along with a Lorentz scale parameter of \(\sigma_{\text{Lorentz}} =0.011\) yr-1 indicating that the underlying oscillation is moderately irregular.

title: Knitting Paper
text:
I’m a passionate knitter and have developed the script below as a tool to support the design of knitting patterns. It can be especially helpful for shaping tasks such as raglan constructions or calculating stitch decreases for necklines. The script is based on your personal knitting gauge—you can adjust the measured values by modifying the variables no_of_stitches_per_10_cm and no_of_rows_per_10_cm. At the end of the script, you’ll also find a few lines of code for printing the knitting paper at the correct scale. Please note that the resulting pdf might have very unusual sizes which might require additional adjustments. Feel free to use, adapt, and improve the R script to suit your own projects.

# R packages needed
packages <- c("ggplot2", "dplyr", "tibble", "grid", "gtable", "tidyr")

# Install packages not yet installed
installed_packages <- packages %in% rownames(installed.packages())
if (any(installed_packages == FALSE)) {
  install.packages(packages[!installed_packages])
}

# Loading packages
invisible(lapply(packages, library, character.only = TRUE))


# gauge result
no_of_stitches_per_10_cm <- 26.5
no_of_rows_per_10_cm <- 37

# size of the output
size_x_cm <- 25
size_y_cm <- 17

output_cm <- tibble(x = c(0, size_x_cm), y= c(0, size_y_cm))

# size of a single stitch in x and y direction
delta_x <- 10/no_of_stitches_per_10_cm
delta_y <- 10/no_of_rows_per_10_cm

total_no_of_stitches <- round(size_x_cm / delta_x)
total_no_of_rows <- round(size_y_cm / delta_y)

#  grid defined by stitch size
lines_v <- tibble(x = (0 : total_no_of_stitches) * delta_x)
lines_h <- tibble(y = (0 : total_no_of_rows) * delta_y)

#  support grid for 10x10 cm
lines_v_dm <- tibble(x = seq(0, size_x_cm, by = 10))
lines_h_dm <- tibble(y = seq(0, size_y_cm, by = 10))

#  support grid for 10x10 stitches and lables
lines_h_10_stitches<- tibble(x = (1:floor(total_no_of_stitches / 10)) * 10 * delta_x)
lines_v_10_stitches  <- tibble(y = (1:floor(total_no_of_rows / 10)) * 10 * delta_y)

lines_v_10_stitches_labels <- lines_v_10_stitches %>%
  mutate(
    label = round(y/ delta_y) ) %>% 
  crossing(tibble(x = seq(15, total_no_of_stitches, by = 30) * delta_x))


# tibble for alternating rows 
alternating_rows <- tibble(
  ymin = (seq(0, total_no_of_rows, by = 2)) * delta_y,
  ymax = ymin + delta_y,
  xmin = 0,
  xmax = delta_x * total_no_of_stitches)


# knitting paper plot

output_cm %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_blank() +
  
  geom_rect(
    data = alternating_rows,
    aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = ymin, ymax = ymax),
    fill = "grey80",
    color = NA,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  
  geom_vline(data = lines_v, aes(xintercept = x), color = "grey60", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_hline(data = lines_h, aes(yintercept = y), color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.4) +
  
  geom_vline(data = lines_h_10_stitches, aes(xintercept = x), color = "grey60", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(data = lines_v_10_stitches, aes(yintercept = y), color = "grey60", linewidth = 0.6) +
  
  geom_vline(data = lines_v_dm, aes(xintercept = x), color = "darkred", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_hline(data = lines_h_dm, aes(yintercept = y), color = "darkred", linewidth = 0.7) +

 
  geom_text(
    data = lines_v_10_stitches_labels,
    aes(x = x,  y = y, label = label),
    col = "grey40", 
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    hjust = 0.5,
    vjust = 0.5,
    size = 3
  ) +

coord_fixed(xlim = c(0, size_x_cm), ylim = c(0, size_y_cm), ratio = 1) +
  
  
 scale_x_continuous(
  name = "Width (cm)",
  breaks = lines_v_dm$x,
  minor_breaks = seq(0, size_x_cm, by =1),
  labels = round(lines_v_dm$x),
  expand = expansion(add = 1*delta_x),
  sec.axis = sec_axis(
    ~ . / delta_x,
    name = "Stitch Number",
    breaks = seq(0, total_no_of_stitches, by = 10 * floor(floor(total_no_of_stitches/10)/4))
  )
) +

scale_y_continuous(
  name = "Length (cm)",
  breaks = lines_h_dm$y,
  labels = round(lines_h_dm$y),
  expand = expansion(add = 1*delta_y),
  sec.axis = sec_axis(
    ~ . / delta_y,
    name = "Row Number",
    breaks = seq(0, total_no_of_rows, by = 10 * floor(floor(total_no_of_rows/10)/4))
  ) 
) +
 

  labs( title  = "Knitting Paper", 
  subtitle = paste(no_of_stitches_per_10_cm, "Stitches per 10 cm/", no_of_rows_per_10_cm, "Rows per 10 cm"), 
  caption = "Enjoy Knitting!\n Origami Data Science Services\n") +
    
  theme(
        title =element_text(size = 18, face = 'bold'),
        
        axis.title.x = element_text(color = "darkred", size = 15 ),
        axis.title.y = element_text(color = "darkred", size = 15),   
        axis.title.x.top = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 15),    
        axis.title.y.right = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 15),  
        
        axis.text.x = element_text(color = "darkred", size = 12),
        axis.text.y = element_text(color = "darkred", size = 12), 
        axis.text.x.top = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 12), 
        axis.text.y.right = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 12), 
        
        
  ) -> plot_knitting_pattern

# Plot of knitting paper

plot_knitting_pattern




Knitting paper based on gauge results



#
## Print knitting pattern into a pdf file
## Sizes of the pdf file correspond to a 1:1 scale with regard to size_x_cm and size_y_cm
#
# g <- ggplotGrob(plot_knitting_pattern)
## Identify panel 
# panel_index <- g$layout$name == "panel"
#
## fix the size of the plot
# g$widths[g$layout$l[panel_index]] <- unit(size_x_cm, "cm")
# g$heights[g$layout$t[panel_index]] <- unit(size_y_cm, "cm")
# total_width  <- sum(g$widths)
# total_height <- sum(g$heights)
#
## pfd with knitting pattern
## BE AWARE OF UNUSUAL PAPER SIZES!
#
## ggsave("knitting_paper_1_to_1.pdf",
#       plot = g,
#       width = convertWidth(total_width, "cm", valueOnly = TRUE),
#       height = convertHeight(total_height, "cm", valueOnly = TRUE),
#       units = "cm",
#       device = "pdf")
